opp.name: Lubrizol [S+P2P] June'26
opp.next_step: Grant to socialize the Zycus deck internally with procurement transformation/SAP stakeholders, and reconnect in a couple of weeks to determine appropriate stakeholders, where to go deeper and next step.
opp.ais_why:
• Lubrizol is 'undergoing a global SAP S/4HANA transformation, interested in Zycus for strategic sourcing and P2P without disrupting ERP' ‚Äî the priority is ERP-compatible S2P, not AI.
• Only Merlin is scoped and the Agentic-platform-discussed flag is not set, with no AI language in the discovery notes.
• Next step is for the sponsor to 'socialize Zycus deck internally,' indicating early internal selling with AI absent as a driver.

items.quote: I know and work with the people that are kind of in charge of some of our procurement transformation efforts.

items.area: No quantified value case / metrics from the buyer
Single-thread: only Grant Lippincott engaged; zero buyer contact roles on the opportunity

metrics.narrative: No quantified value case exists yet. The 18 Jun 2026 intro call was educational and contained no numbers from Lubrizol on current procurement cost, cycle time, savings target, or ROI. Zycus presented its own proof points (Delta Airlines '600% ROI', PwC consolidating 65,000+ users) but the buyer did not articulate a metric of their own. Grant did confirm relevant context: SAP S/4 standardization is underway and they have ~1,500 employees globally (Grant, 18 Jun 2026). The next move is to quantify the Capex procurement pain (cycle time, leakage, supplier-onboarding effort) with Grant or a transformation lead before any commercial conversation.

champion.narrative: Grant Lippincott is a developing, friendly contact but not yet a proven champion. Positives: he was highly engaged ('very interested to learn more, I have so many questions'), volunteered to socialize the deck with procurement-transformation and SAP leads, offered to try to pull those stakeholders into follow-up, and has a warm personal hook (his father works at Panasonic, a Zycus customer that replaced Ariba) (Grant, 18 Jun 2026). What is still missing: he openly questioned whether he is even the right owner ('if it's not me that's the right person for it'), has no demonstrated access to budget or the economic buyer, and is currently the sole engaged contact. Strength is developing, not strong, until he produces an internal introduction.

competition.narrative: SAP Ariba is the incumbent and the only named competitor. Lubrizol runs SAP as its ERP and is standardizing on S/4 globally; Grant confirmed 'we use Ariba' but added 'I'm not sure what the long term answer is there' (Grant, 18 Jun 2026), signaling openness rather than a committed replacement. Zycus positioned its wedge directly: single organically-built database vs Ariba's multi-product stack, SAP S/4 certified out-of-the-box adapters (one of only two such vendors per Ed Dlugosz), Ariba's stalled enhancements as customers are pushed to S/4, and the Panasonic-replaced-Ariba reference. The competitive risk is not a rival win but scope shrink: Grant noted some tools were 'recently changed and kind of standardized on', so Lubrizol may keep those and adopt Zycus only piecemeal. No other vendor (Coupa, Ivalua, GEP, Zip) was named.

identify_pain.narrative: Pain is implied and partially confirmed but not yet owned commercially. Grant engaged strongly on field-user access for Capex approvals (the MS Teams conversational bot), complex Capex sourcing (RFIs/RFPs/RFQs), supplier onboarding, change-order handling, and reporting/budget visibility — confirming these are relevant to how Lubrizol runs Capex procurement (Grant, 18 Jun 2026). He also flagged a live driver: an enterprise SAP S/4 standardization program where Zycus could layer on or replace tools. Confirmed pain is qualitative (field access, Capex complexity, fragmented tooling); the financial impact is inferred, not stated. Pain owner today is Grant for Capex; the broader transformation pain owner is unnamed.

paper_process.narrative: No contracting, legal, procurement-channel, or InfoSec detail has been discussed — expected for a deal 12 days old that is still educational. The only adjacent signal is that any solution must coexist with an active enterprise SAP S/4 standardization initiative (Grant, 18 Jun 2026), which will eventually pull IT and integration governance into the paper process. Visibility will come once an internal sponsor and a scoped project exist.

economic_buyer.narrative: No economic buyer is identified. Grant Lippincott described himself as the 'Capex lead on the procurement side' who manages the US team and supports the global team (Grant, 18 Jun 2026) — a functional lead, not a budget owner, and there was no pricing or budget discussion. Grant pointed to a separate group, the 'people that are kind of in charge of some of our procurement transformation efforts', as the likely owners but named no one. Budget authority is therefore unknown. The move is to use Grant's internal socialization to surface and get a name for the procurement-transformation/budget owner.

decision_process.narrative: There is no defined decision process; Grant confirmed 'definitely educational at this point' with no formal project (Grant, 18 Jun 2026). The only process step is Grant's stated plan to socialize the deck internally with procurement-transformation and SAP stakeholders and reconnect in roughly two weeks (i.e. by ~mid-July 2026) (SF NextStep; Grant, 18 Jun 2026). Approval chain, evaluation stages, and timeline are unknown. The move is to map who Grant talks to internally and turn the reconnect into a mutual next-step plan.

decision_criteria.narrative: Early criteria surfaced from Grant's own questions rather than a formal evaluation. He probed SAP S/4 integration ('we have SAP as our ERP system... on S/4'), the ability to adopt the suite piecemeal ('is it possible to piecemeal some portions of the suite where we maybe would be interested in parts but not others?'), and implementation duration (answered 6-9 months) (Grant, 18 Jun 2026). These imply criteria of SAP-native integration, modular/coexistence flexibility, and reasonable time-to-value. None are confirmed as formal evaluation criteria — there is no RFP or scorecard. The move is to convert these into a documented requirement set in the next session.

deal_movement.summary: Brand-new opportunity created 18 Jun 2026 off a strong educational intro call the same day. Last Salesforce activity 18 Jun 2026 (12 days ago); last modified 30 Jun 2026. Buyer committed to internal socialization and a reconnect in ~2 weeks (by ~mid-July 2026).

confidence_signals.summary: One strong, recent buyer touchpoint (18 Jun 2026, ~12 days ago) with active interest; buyer committed to internal socialization and a reconnect. Engagement is live but single-threaded.

north_star_verdict.math: Created 18 Jun 2026 (12 days ago), stage Qualified, single buyer call on 18 Jun 2026. Close date 31 Mar 2027 is 274 days out. For a 12-15 month enterprise S2P cycle this is realistic. Only one contact engaged (Grant Lippincott), and the buyer explicitly framed this as 'definitely educational' (Grant, 18 Jun 2026), so the deal is at the very front of the funnel but moving normally for its age.
evidence: Buyer call held 18 Jun 2026 (~12 days ago); Grant Lippincott engaged actively and asked detailed product questions (Avoma intro call 18 Jun 2026).
Grant: 'definitely educational at this point' — no formal project yet (Avoma intro call 18 Jun 2026).
Forecast category Pipeline matches an early Qualified-stage education deal (SF ForecastCategory).
north_star_verdict.headline: Brand-new early-stage education deal: strong intro call 18 Jun 2026, buyer engaged, close date 274 days out is plausible for an enterprise S2P cycle.

flags: Single-threaded: Grant Lippincott is the only engaged stakeholder; no EB, DM, champion, or multi-person buying committee visible. Grant's internal-sell success determines whether deal progresses or stalls.

Establish a quantified business case: the call was all-education with zero buyer metrics; in the next session anchor a cost/cycle-time/leakage number for Capex procurement so the deal can move past capability discussion to a funded project.
best_practice_check.summary: A healthy, brand-new education deal whose biggest win-rate levers are converting the warm Capex contact into a multi-threaded transformation conversation and quantifying the pain before competitors do.

competitive_position.summary: SAP Ariba is the incumbent and the single, current competitive reference as of 18 Jun 2026. It is a displacement target, not an active bake-off: Lubrizol runs SAP/S4 and uses Ariba, but Grant is uncertain on Ariba's long-term role ('I'm not sure what the long term answer is there'). Zycus's wedge is single-database architecture, SAP S/4-certified integration, and Ariba's stalled roadmap, reinforced by the Panasonic-replaced-Ariba reference. No other vendor named.

Multi-thread urgently: Grant is a strong champion but not the Economic Buyer. He committed to socialize internally with procurement transformation and SAP stakeholders by early July; the deal team must secure introductions to those leads (likely Director or VP level) within the two-week window to map budget authority, confirm the S2P evaluation is real (vs exploratory), and align with the SAP S/4 program timeline, which may dictate the S2P decision and governance. A single-thread deal that stays with Grant alone will stall at education and never reach budget/project approval.

Activate the Panasonic reference immediately: Grant volunteered to ask his father (Panasonic employee) about their recent Zycus rollout and Ariba replacement. This is a rare, unencumbered peer reference that can validate the Ariba-to-Zycus path and surface any shared pain (S/4 migration complexity, Ariba end-of-life risk, implementation experience). Follow up within 7 days to learn what Grant's father shared, capture any Panasonic contact willing to speak directly, and use that proof point when Grant multi-threads to transformation leads to de-risk the Ariba replacement decision.

Position Horizon user group (21–23 Sep, Beaver Creek) as the next forcing function: Grant expressed genuine interest ('I love Colorado. I would love to... those opportunities are incredibly valuable. It's nice to network with others that are using these systems') but deferred commitment pending internal buy-in. If Grant successfully multi-threads and procurement transformation leads engage, the user group becomes a low-risk, high-value next step to expose multiple Lubrizol stakeholders (transformation lead, SAP program lead, Capex team) to Gartner/Forrester analysts, Zycus executives, and 150+ peer customers (Heineken 72-country rollout, PWC 100k users, Delta 600% ROI, Panasonic Ariba replacement). Drive for 2–3 Lubrizol attendees by mid-August to build stakeholder consensus and peer validation before any RFI/RFP process begins.

Drive for 2–3 Lubrizol attendees by mid-August to build stakeholder consensus and peer validation before any RFI/RFP process begins.

value.flag: Multi-thread urgently: Grant is a strong champion but not the Economic Buyer. He committed to socialize internally with procurement transformation and SAP stakeholders by early July; the deal team must secure introductions to those leads (likely Director or VP level) within the two-week window to map budget authority, confirm the S2P evaluation is real (vs exploratory), and align with the SAP S/4 program timeline, which may dictate the S2P decision and governance. A single-thread deal that stays with Grant alone will stall at education and never reach budget/project approval.

value.flag: Activate the Panasonic reference immediately: Grant volunteered to ask his father (Panasonic employee) about their recent Zycus rollout and Ariba replacement. This is a rare, unencumbered peer reference that can validate the Ariba-to-Zycus path and surface any shared pain (S/4 migration complexity, Ariba end-of-life risk, implementation experience). Follow up within 7 days to learn what Grant's father shared, capture any Panasonic contact willing to speak directly, and use that proof point when Grant multi-threads to transformation leads to de-risk the Ariba replacement decision.
